notes: Bury the rhizome and it rots; tie to hardscape. Extremely forgiving of low light and low-CO2 tanks. Prone to black-beard algae on old leaves in high-flow spots.

origin: Cultivar — Oriental Aquarium Singapore (from Cameroon parent)
notes: Trademarked cultivar by Oriental Aquarium; leaves ~1-2 cm. Slower and smaller than Nana — lifetime of a single leaf can exceed a year. Excellent for nano and shrimp tanks.

pinyin: ka luo lai na guo chang sha
notes: Thick succulent stems emit a lemon scent when crushed. Tolerates hard water and low CO2 but colors brighter copper under 50+ µmol/m²/s PAR.

One of the most cosmopolitan aquatic plants; sold in virtually every fish store worldwide under the name 'moneywort'. Adapts readily to hard or soft water. Emersed-to-submersed conversion is smooth — no melting phase. Tiny round leaves are brighter green than B. caroliniana and lack the lemon scent.

pinyin: la jiao rong ke da gang
hong huo yan la jiao rong
notes: Like Anubias, rhizome must stay above substrate. Kedagang shows deep green leaves with red undersides and occasional white flowers. Melting is common for 4-6 weeks after import from emersed — trim melting leaves, new growth follows.

origin: Eastern North America, Central America, South America
notes: Fan-shaped finely divided leaves make it a classic shoaling-fish background. Dislikes hard alkaline water — KH above 6 causes leaf disintegration at the tips. Invasive in several regions outside its native range; do not release into open water. Cool-water plant — below 26°C is preferable.
Expect 'crypt melt' after any major water-parameter change — leaves dissolve, rhizome survives, new growth follows in 2-3 weeks. Nutrient-rich substrate strongly recommended.

Leaves start olive-green then develop crimson speckles under strong light and iron-rich substrate. Heavy root feeder — root tabs every 2-3 months accelerate color expression. Often sold as E. 'Ozelot Red' relative.

True E. parvula tops out at ~6-10 cm; E. acicularis sold as 'mini' is often taller. Dense carpet needs trimming at 2-3 cm to prevent lower-layer melting. CO2 injection dramatically shortens runner spread time.
Grows upward and loses carpet form below ~100 µmol/m²/s PAR — ADA Nature Aquarium classic precisely because it demands Solar-I-grade light. Heat above 27°C stresses it quickly.

Large-leaved workhorse for background planting in low-tech and high-tech tanks alike. Multiple cultivars exist: 'Kompakt' stays compact at 20 cm; 'Siamensis' has narrower leaves. Emersed form develops bronze-purple leaves and large corymbs of purple flowers. Robust nitrogen uptake makes it an excellent tank cycling assistant.
Federally prohibited in the US (USDA noxious weed) — do NOT ship to US customers. Extremely fast starter plant; tolerates low tech. Trim aggressively to avoid shading neighbors.

Gets atmospheric CO2 directly from air — k_co2 is low by design; do not interpret as CO2-injection-required. Keep leaves dry by running surface skimmer off during photoperiod. Excellent nitrate sponge; thin weekly.

origin: South and Southeast Asia — India, Japan, China, Philippines
notes: Feathery whorled leaves create a soft, delicate texture in the midground/background. Closely resembles Limnophila aquatica but is notably smaller. Very forgiving of low-tech conditions; a classic beginner stem plant. Often confused with Cabomba due to similar leaf shape — distinction is simple: Limnophila has whorled leaves on a single axis, Cabomba has fan-branched leaves.

Compact companion to L. repens; smaller leaf size (0.5–2 cm) makes it suit midground placement better. Red underside develops reliably without CO2 injection under moderate to high PAR. The cosmopolitan distribution is unusually broad for a Ludwigia — native on six continents. Tolerates cooler water better than most tropical stem plants.

pinyin: pan ta na er hong hu die
pan ta na er ding xiang
notes: Considered one of the most demanding stem plants in the hobby. Needs aggressive fertilization (EI-level Fe, PO4, NO3) and pristine water. Melts under kH > 3 or temperature shifts — calibration note: narrow temp_tolerance_c reflects this. Ludwigia sp. 'Pantanal' is taxonomically unresolved.

origin: Southern United States, Mexico, Central America
notes: Classic beginner red stem. Under medium light and no CO2 stays olive-green; red color requires Fe dosing + PAR > 60 µmol/m²/s. Frequently confused with L. glandulosa ('Perennis').

Produces single-lobe leaves under high light/CO2, classic 4-leaf clover under moderate conditions. Slower carpet than Monte Carlo but more forgiving of low-tech tanks. Aquatic fern — propagates via stolons not seeds.

pinyin: meng te ka luo zhen zhu cao
notes: Larger leaf and more forgiving than HC Cuba. Commonly misidentified as Hemianthus in the trade. Pearls vigorously at 25+ ppm CO2. Trim early and often — lower layers yellow if shaded.
Like standard Java fern but leaves 0.5-1 cm wide × 15-20 cm long. Rhizome must stay above substrate. 'Java fern melt' (blackening leaf edges) indicates nutrient deficiency, often K or N, not disease.

pinyin: ma tuo ge luo suo hu wei
notes: Finely pinnate leaves arranged in whorls of 4–6; very similar in appearance to M. pinnatum. Under CO2 injection and strong light, stems pearl profusely and take on a reddish tint at the growing tip. Requires soft, slightly acidic water for best colour — performs poorly in hard tap water above KH 8. Trim regularly to prevent lower leaf drop.

True litmus test of a high-tech tank. Shade intolerance is severe — anything above it melts the lower leaves. Drops leaves with any CO2 interruption > 2 hours. Consider lean nitrogen (< 10 ppm NO3) for deepest red.

Calibration reference species (k_photosynthesis = 1.00). Pink flush under high light + lean NO3 (5-10 ppm). Melts during submersed transition then re-emerges. R. indica is a distinct species sometimes mis-traded as this.
Short bushy stem — frequently shelved as a rosette in retail, hence group = 'rosette' while growthForm = 'stem'. Top and re-plant to create a dense low hedge; side shoots multiply quickly under CO2.
Historically mislabeled as Vesicularia dubyana. Tolerates anything short of scalding temperatures; growth becomes stringy above 27°C. Trim on a schedule to prevent detritus traps.

Named for triangular pinnate branching that resembles a fir tree. More temperature-sensitive than Java moss — melts above 27°C. Best attached to driftwood or mesh; CO2 accelerates the characteristic compact form.

The smallest Cryptocoryne species — leaves rarely exceed 5 cm even under ideal conditions. Unlike other crypts it does not substantially change leaf shape between emersed and submersed forms, so 'crypt melt' is mild. Excellent foreground carpeting in low to medium light with CO2.

Produces dramatically long, narrow leaves with strongly crinkled margins — a distinctive background plant. Tolerates moderate currents. Like most crypts it is a heavy root feeder; root tabs significantly improve growth rate and leaf texture.

origin: North and Central America, South America
notes: Spreads via stolons to form dense foreground carpets. Under intense light and CO2 the narrow leaves develop reddish coloration. Avoid alkaline hard water — leaves become chlorotic above pH 7.5. A smaller, carpeting alternative to larger Amazon sword species.

pinyin: wen de luo fu tie huang guan
origin: Cultivar — Tropica, Denmark (from Southeast Asian parent)
notes: Trademarked Tropica cultivar with finely forked leaf tips giving a lace-like appearance. Attach to rock or driftwood — burying the rhizome causes rot. Adventitious plantlets form on older leaves. Extremely tolerant and beginner-friendly despite its delicate appearance.

Pinnate semi-transparent leaves with a brilliant emerald sheen under good current. Requires CO2 and moderate light — stagnant soft water is the key requirement. Attach to rocks or wood with thread or glue; do not bury the rhizome. Hard water causes rapid deterioration.

pinyin: mi ni shan hu mo si
notes: A liverwort, not a true moss — classified as a hepatic bryophyte. Forms dense, coral-like cushions highly valued in Nature aquascape compositions. Requires CO2 and good flow; stagnant conditions lead to algae smothering. Sensitive to temperature above 26°C.

Distinctive two-ranked 'pocket' leaf arrangement differentiates it from all other aquatic mosses. Prefers cooler water — above 24°C growth stalls. Attach to hardscape with glue; anchoring thread works but is tedious given its fine structure. In aquascaping often sold as 'Phoenix Moss'.

One of the fastest-growing and hardiest aquatic mosses. Long, wispy strands drape beautifully over hardscape or attach loosely to mesh. Excellent refuge for fry and shrimp. Requires regular pruning to prevent light-blocking mat formation.

Rosette-shaped floating plant that aggressively exports nitrogen and phosphorus via its extensive dangling root system — a popular biological filter. Sensitive to water splashing on leaves which causes rot. Invasive in tropical open water; never release outdoors. Long roots serve as spawning substrate and fry cover.

A true aquatic fern with two oval floating leaves covered in water-repelling hairs and one submerged root-like leaf. Naturalised across Eurasia but also widely cultivated. Provides shade for lower-light plants and excellent diffused light effect. Can double in biomass within a week under strong light — thin regularly.
